recap: Error intervals show the range of values a rounded number could have been — lower bound uses <=, upper bound uses <, and each bound is half the rounding unit away from the rounded value.

questions.question: A number, n, is rounded to the nearest 10 and gives 50. Write down the error interval for n.
A length, l, is measured as 3.6 cm to 1 decimal place. Write down the error interval for l.
A time, t, is recorded as 48 seconds to the nearest 2 seconds. Write down the error interval for t.

steps: Degree of accuracy = 2 seconds. Half = 1 second.

questions.question: A number is rounded to 2 decimal places and gives 6.43. Write down the error interval. Then explain whether 6.425 could be the original number.

steps: 6.425 is exactly the lower bound — the interval uses <=, so 6.425 is included. Yes, it could be the original number.
solution.final_answer: 6.425 <= n < 6.435. Yes, 6.425 could be the original value as the lower bound is included.

questions.question: A rectangle has length 8.5 cm and width 4.3 cm, both measured to 1 decimal place. Find the upper bound of the area of the rectangle.

summary: Error interval: lower bound <= value < upper bound.
Each bound is half the rounding unit either side of the rounded value.
Use <= for the lower bound (included) and < for the upper bound (not included).
For compound calculations involving bounds, use upper bounds for both measurements to find the maximum result.
